does: This paper addresses real-time optimization of sorter diversion in high-volume warehouse material handling systems by comparing three machine learning approaches: linear regression with gradient descent, XGBoost with Bayesian optimization, and Bayesian contextual bandits. The key contribution is demonstrating that Bayesian contextual bandits can dynamically adapt diversion control policies to time-varying operational contexts—including volume mode, congestion levels, equipment status, and system dependencies—where traditional static weight configurations fail. The authors develop a physics-aware emulator to enable safe offline training and cold-start resolution, then conduct empirical comparisons to evaluate which framework best balances exploration and exploitation in the real warehouse setting.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research in contextual bandits and reinforcement learning for resource allocation, extending these techniques to the practical domain of warehouse automation and operations research. It connects to prior studies on Bayesian optimization for complex systems and contextual multi-armed bandit theory, particularly methods for handling exploration-exploitation tradeoffs in high-dimensional decision spaces. The paper also draws from work on physics-informed machine learning and digital twins for industrial systems, using simulation-based training to bridge the sim-to-real gap. Additionally, it relates to broader literature on adaptive control in material handling systems and dynamic routing in logistics networks, where static policies increasingly fail as operational complexity grows.
enables: This research opens pathways for deploying adaptive learning systems in large-scale warehouse networks, potentially extending contextual bandit frameworks to multi-objective optimization across interconnected material handling nodes. The physics-aware emulator approach enables future work on safer, sample-efficient deployment of learning agents in other critical logistics infrastructure. Results could drive development of hybrid offline-online learning pipelines that reduce risks in transitioning autonomous systems to real operations. The comparative framework provides a foundation for investigating how contextual bandits scale to higher-dimensional state spaces and longer planning horizons, and for applying similar methods to other time-sensitive optimization problems in supply chain automation where real-time adaptation to changing conditions is essential.
